text:
MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 63-year-old female with known stage IIIB (now stage IV) left lung adenocarcinoma, EGFR L858R-mutant, on osimertinib (Tagrisso) since approximately 14 months prior. Recent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis demonstrated enlargement of the left upper lobe primary mass, new left hilar lymphadenopathy, and a new right adrenal nodule, consistent with systemic progression. MRI brain obtained to evaluate for intracranial metastatic disease in the setting of systemic progression.

COMPARISON: MRI brain with and without gadolinium performed approximately 5 months prior (9-month surveillance study), which was unremarkable.

TECHNIQUE: Multiplanar, multisequence MRI of the brain was performed on a 3-Tesla scanner without and with intravenous gadolinium contrast (15 mL of gadobutrol administered without adverse event). Sequences obtained include sagittal T1, axial T2, axial FLAIR, axial diffusion-weighted imaging with ADC map, axial susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI), axial pre- and post-gadolinium T1-weighted imaging, and post-contrast 3D T1 MPRAGE with multiplanar reformats.

FINDINGS:

Supratentorial Brain:
There are three new foci of abnormal contrast enhancement identified on this examination that were not present on the prior MRI brain from approximately 5 months ago.

The largest is a rounded, well-circumscribed area of homogeneous enhancement in the right frontal lobe, white matter, measuring approximately 1.4 cm in greatest axial dimension. This lesion demonstrates a thin rim of surrounding T2/FLAIR signal hyperintensity consistent with mild vasogenic edema. There is no significant mass effect on the adjacent sulci or midline structures. No associated restricted diffusion to suggest acute ischemia. Susceptibility-weighted imaging shows no evidence of intratumoral hemorrhage.

A second lesion is identified in the right parietal lobe at the gray-white matter junction, measuring approximately 0.8 cm. This lesion is also well-circumscribed with solid nodular enhancement. The surrounding FLAIR signal change is modest. No hemorrhagic component on SWI.

Infratentorial Brain / Posterior Fossa:
A third focus of enhancement is identified within the left cerebellar hemisphere, also at the gray-white matter junction, measuring approximately 0.6 cm. Enhancement is nodular and homogeneous. There is minimal surrounding edema. No hydrocephalus or obstructive changes to the fourth ventricle. The cerebellar tonsils are in normal position without evidence of downward herniation.

Leptomeninges:
No abnormal leptomeningeal enhancement is identified. No sulcal or cisternal enhancement pattern to suggest carcinomatous meningitis.

Ventricular System and CSF Spaces:
The lateral ventricles, third ventricle, and fourth ventricle are normal in size and configuration. No hydrocephalus. The sulci and cisterns are widely patent and age-appropriate.

Calvarium and Skull Base:
No abnormal calvarial or skull base marrow signal alteration or enhancement to suggest osseous metastatic involvement.

Sella and Parasellar Region:
Unremarkable. No pituitary mass. Cavernous sinuses are symmetric.

Vascular Structures:
The major intracranial arterial flow voids are preserved on the non-contrast sequences. No definite arteriovenous malformation or vascular abnormality identified.

White Matter:
Scattered nonspecific foci of T2/FLAIR signal hyperintensity in the periventricular and subcortical white matter, mild in degree, consistent with chronic small vessel ischemic change in the context of the patient's known hypertension and type 2 diabetes. These changes are stable and unchanged from the prior study.

IMPRESSION:

1. Three new parenchymal enhancing lesions identified since the prior MRI brain performed approximately 5 months ago, representing new intracranial metastatic disease. The lesions are located in the right frontal lobe (1.4 cm), right parietal lobe (0.8 cm), and left cerebellar hemisphere (0.6 cm). Each is at or near the gray-white matter junction, a distribution highly characteristic of hematogenous metastatic spread. Mild surrounding vasogenic edema accompanies the right frontal lesion; the remaining lesions have minimal associated edema.

2. No evidence of leptomeningeal carcinomatosis.

3. No intracranial hemorrhage, herniation, or obstructive hydrocephalus.

4. Stable mild white matter changes consistent with chronic small vessel ischemic disease related to underlying vascular risk factors.

The overall intracranial metastatic burden is limited (three lesions, all under 1.5 cm, without significant mass effect), and the findings may be suitable for consideration of focal stereotactic intervention. Neurosurgery and/or radiation oncology correlation is warranted.
